1: Psalm 60 has the most historically detailed superscription in the Psalter: it links to the campaigns of 2 Samuel 8:3–13 and 1 Chronicles 18:3, 12–13, in which David fought the Aramean kingdoms and Joab defeated twelve thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt (south of the Dead Sea). The psalm is unusual among Davidic psalms in being a community lament after a military defeat — evidence that not all of David's campaigns were immediately victorious. "Lily of the Testimony" (šûšan ʿēdût) is a tune title; the word ʿēdût (testimony, covenant law) is also used for the ark of the covenant (ʾārôn hāʿēdût), giving the title a covenantal resonance. Psalm 60 is largely duplicated in Psalms 108:6–13, showing that individual psalms were reused in new collections and contexts.
2: "You have made the earth shake; you have cracked it open; heal its fractures, for it is collapsing" — the earthquake (hirʿaštāh ʾereṣ, you made the earth shake) is the standard language of theophany and divine judgment in the OT (Psalms 18:7; Psalms 29:8; Psalms 68:8; Psalms 77:18; Habakkuk 3:10). Applied to military defeat, the metaphor is precise: the defeat felt as if the very ground of national security had cracked open. "Heal its fractures" (rĕpāʾāh šĕbārêhā) — the same healing language applied to a broken land as to a broken person (Psalms 147:3: "he heals the brokenhearted and binds up their wounds"). The petition for healing the land echoes the Solomonic prayer (2 Chronicles 7:14: "if my people who are called by my name humble themselves... then I will heal their land").
3: "You have put your people through severe trials; you have made us drink disorienting wine" — yên tarʿēlāh (wine of staggering/reeling). In the prophetic literature, the "cup" that YHWH hands the nations or Israel to drink is a standard metaphor for judgment and catastrophe (Isaiah 51:17–22; Jeremiah 25:15–29; Ezekiel 23:31–34; Habakkuk 2:16; Revelation 16:19). The wine does not intoxicate with pleasure but with confusion and loss of capacity — the military defeat has left the community disoriented, unable to assess the situation clearly. "You have put your people through severe trials" (hirʾîtānû qāšôt) — the acknowledgment that the testing came from God, not merely from the enemy, is theologically significant: it frames the defeat as covenant discipline rather than divine abandonment.
4: "You have set up a banner for those who fear you, for them to rally to in the face of the enemy's bow" — nēs (battle standard, signal flag) was the pole or marker around which dispersed troops or people were to gather (Isaiah 5:26; Psalms 11:10, 12; Psalms 62:10; Jeremiah 4:21). The military metaphor: in the chaos of battle, the standard shows troops where the rallying point is. The prayer is that YHWH has established such a banner — that the defeat has not scattered the community beyond recovery but that God has provided a visible gathering point. "Those who fear you (lĕyirʾêkā)" — the covenant community defined by yirʾat YHWH, used throughout the Psalter as the hallmark of the faithful.
5: "Save your dear ones — rescue them by your right hand, and answer our prayer" — yĕdîdêkā (your beloved ones, your dear ones) — the term for those who stand in a relationship of special affection with YHWH. The same word characterizes Benjamin as "beloved of YHWH" (Deuteronomy 33:12) and Solomon as Yĕdîdyāh (loved by YHWH, 2 Samuel 12:25). "Your right hand" (yĕmînĕkā) is the instrument of divine power and victory (Exodus 15:6, 12; Psalms 18:35; Psalms 44:3; Psalms 98:1). The appeal combines affection (beloved ones) and power (right hand) as the two grounds for rescue — YHWH acts because he loves Israel and because he has the power to save.
6: "God has spoken in his holiness: 'I will triumph; I will divide up Shechem and portion out the Valley of Succoth'" — dibber bĕqodšô (God has spoken in his holiness/sanctuary). The oracle begins: YHWH speaks from his sanctuary (qōdeš can be either the divine attribute of holiness or the sanctuary as its location — probably the former here, giving the oracle divine-character authority). The oracle asserts YHWH's territorial sovereignty over the promised land: Shechem (the central highland city, site of covenant assemblies in Josh 24) and Succoth (in the Transjordan Jordan valley, east of the Jordan) together represent the breadth of the land — west and east of the Jordan.
7: "'Gilead is mine, Manasseh is mine; Ephraim is my helmet; Judah is my scepter'" — the oracle continues distributing the territories. Gilead (Transjordan highland) and Manasseh (the largest tribe, split across both sides of the Jordan) bracket the Transjordanian territory. Ephraim (the most powerful northern tribe) is YHWH's "helmet" (māʿôz rōʾšî, literally "stronghold of my head") — the military protection of the divine warrior's head, possibly referring to Ephraim's geographical position protecting the north. Judah is YHWH's "scepter" (mĕḥōqĕqî, my lawgiver/ruler's staff) — the royal tribe through which the covenant line of authority runs (Genesis 49:10: "the scepter will not depart from Judah").
8: "'Moab is my washbasin; over Edom I throw my sandal; over Philistia I shout in triumph'" — the three neighboring peoples who were traditional enemies of Israel are reduced to servile objects. Moab as a "washbasin" (sîr raḥṣî) — a servant's vessel for washing feet, the most menial of household implements. Edom: the sandal-throw has two possible meanings: (1) asserting ownership by throwing a sandal onto property (cf. Psalms 108:9, Ruth 4:7–8, where the sandal gesture marks a transfer of property rights); (2) contemptuous dismissal — flinging a sandal at someone. Both meanings converge in a gesture of complete domination. Philistia, historically Israel's most persistent military threat, is simply shouted over in triumph (hitroʿaʿtî) — reduced to an object of a victory cry.
9: "Who will bring me into the fortified city? Who will guide me all the way to Edom?" — the "fortified city" (ʿîr māṣôr) almost certainly refers to Sela (Petra), the Edomite rock city in the southern Transjordan — approached through narrow canyon passes and nearly impregnable. This is the capital of the territory YHWH has just claimed in the oracle (Psalms 60:8). The question signals military realism: even with the divine oracle asserting territorial ownership, taking the fortified city requires a guide and divine accompaniment into hostile territory. The prayer moves from oracle (Psalms 60:6–8) to practical military petition (Psalms 60:9–12).
10: "Have you not rejected us, O God? You no longer go out, God, with our armies" — hălōʾ ʾattāh ʾĕlōhîm zĕnaḥtānû wĕlōʾ-tēṣēʾ ʾĕlōhîm bĕṣibʾôtênû. The divine warrior's absence from battle was the most catastrophic possibility in covenant theology (cf. 2 Samuel 5:24, where YHWH's "going out ahead of you" is the signal to attack; and Deuteronomy 20:4, where YHWH's going with the army is the basis for fearlessness in battle). The defeat of Psalms 60:1 is here interpreted as YHWH "not going out" with the armies — the divine military presence withdrawn as a covenant consequence. This is not doubt but honest liturgical wrestling with the experience of divine absence.
11: "Give us help against the enemy, for human deliverance is worthless" — šāwʾ tĕšûʿat ʾādām (vain/empty is the salvation that comes from ʾādām, mortal humans). The word šāwʾ (vanity, emptiness, worthlessness) is the key term of Ecclesiastes (translated "vanity") and appears throughout the Psalter as the characterization of what human strength alone can achieve without divine backing. The military conclusion is that no human alliance, strategy, or strength can succeed where YHWH has withdrawn. This is a radical statement of military theology: the only reliable source of deliverance is the covenant God, and any confidence placed elsewhere is šāwʾ.
12: "Through God we will do great deeds; he himself will crush our enemies" — the psalm ends in confident assertion despite the lament: bĕʾĕlōhîm naʿăśeh ḥāyil wĕhûʾ yābûs ṣārênû. The transition from "God has rejected us" (Psalms 60:10) to "through God we will do great deeds" (Psalms 60:12) is not logically resolved within the psalm — the oracle of Psalms 60:6–8 provides the basis for the confidence, but the defeat of Psalms 60:1–3 is still present. This is the Psalter's characteristic liturgical move: bringing complaint and confidence into proximity without eliminating the tension, trusting that the God who spoke the oracle is the same God who permitted the defeat, and that both are within his governance. The final word is "he himself will crush our enemies" — active divine intervention completing what human strength cannot.
